sentences.text: [INAUDIBLE].
And we had very beautiful two Seders.
They served the usual Passover, all the trimmings.
And started of course with [NON-ENGLISH] and [NON-ENGLISH] and fish and all kinds of meats and all kinds of wonderful nostalgia food.
I hope in-- very soon.
In June, we intend to go to Chicago and to see our children.
Our grandchild is going to be bar-- bat mitzvahed.
And we are very, very excited about that.
And some-- my brother from Israel intends to come and family.
So we will all be together again.
My name is Slava Fintel.
S-L-A-V-A F-I-N-T-E-L.
My maiden name was Cymer, C-Y-M-E-R.
I was born June 30-- June 2, in '30.
Today I am 64.
I was born in a small town, Szarkowszczyzna.
It was in eastern Poland, not far from Vilna.
My father's name Shmuel.
My mother's name was Lebe.
Oh yes, we were seven.
I had two brothers, and with me five sisters.
My oldest sister was Dena, my next was Miriam, and then [NON-ENGLISH], Zalman, Celia, Chaya, and myself.
My father was a merchant, and my two brothers were working with him.
My mother had a restaurant, and she worked very hard, although we had help, live-in help, a couple.
But still, I remember my mother working very, very hard.
Our home.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] [SIGHS] I have such a good memories.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I was the youngest, and it was such a happy home.
My mother belonged to a organization.
It was Bikur Cholim, they called it.
And she used to do a lot of charity work.
I remember we had a very large room and a very big table.
And the table was constantly filled with people who couldn't afford to come to eat.
Our doors were always open.
And lots of laughter, lots of happiness.
And because the city was small, all the people there were involved with each other, either related or just good friends.
And I remember the most beautiful, beautiful childhood.
I was constantly caressed by everybody, especially my mother.
I loved her.
The city was located-- we had a river.
And I loved to play in this river and swim and play and run and-- [SIGHS] when I think of it now, I, I am thinking that this was really the most beautiful time in my life.
And the memories are so vivid, so beautiful.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But this was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] abrupted when the-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] when the world war started.
The Germans attacked Poland.
And after a very short time, instead of the Germans, the Russians came.
Our town, it was-- we had two synagogues.
And it was Ashkanazi and it was Sephardim.
We, we loved to belong to the synagogue.
We weren't very religious, but we were traditional.
Holidays were observed and with lots and lots of people coming and going.
In our house, because it was such a large home, my mother also helped out for very poor people to get married and they would be married in our house.
I remember we had the huge chair where the brides would be seated.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We also had in our house a-- once, like in once a month or so, it would be a movies.
They would come, and we would watch movies.
Also it would be gathering socially for young people, because, as I say, it was a very, very large room.
Our home had very big rooms and many.
In the back of our house, there was another house.
This was built to-- for the future for my two sisters, when-- if they will get married, that they would live there.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I remember also my mother used to send me every Friday with little packages to bring to people who couldn't afford.
She would bake challah.
She would milk the cows and send milk, cheese, and challah.
And I used to come.
Also, she would ask my sister Celia-- she was older than I-- and she went with me.
I had so many friends.
The family was large because there were uncles and aunts and cousins.
And it was an interwoven family, so I never, never felt that I was missing anything.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It was-- [SIGHS] it was so beautiful, although we didn't have any bathroom in the house or running water.
We had an outhouse.
And water was brought in in by-- in barrels, and it was standing in the entrance in a big wooden barrel.
Later on, my father, they could afford and we had a well in the back, which it was done with the bringing out the water in, in buckets.
Electricity came also later.
I remember before we had lamps in every room in the evening, my sisters would go and put up the gasoline in each lamp.
And this how we had light.
But I remember it was such a relation that electricity was brought in.
And I remember hanging that electric bulb in each room.
This was very exciting, because after that, they start also bringing the movies that I mentioned.
And life became much more exciting because of the electricity.
My father loved to read.
And his time, the time that he had left over from working and-- he used to read a lot.
My mother was very busy, but she liked to sew, and she liked to bicycle riding.
And this was such a big thing, because none of the women would dare to indulge in such pleasures.
But she liked it.
And I was constantly around her.
I adored her.

[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [SIGHS] I was very spoiled by my siblings-- by my sisters, by my brother-- because I was the smallest, and I could do [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So when the war broke out, it became very, very-- everybody was so sad and so frightened.
Because nobody knew what the future will bring.
Oh yes.
She was an excellent cook and a baker.
She used to make from grind potatoes, which I loved so much, all kinds of-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] she used to make kugel and latkes, and she baked all kinds of things.
And Saturday morning, we had a huge oven and everything was put inside the oven, like a cholent.
Even the milk for the coffee was put Friday night into it.
And Saturday morning we had that big, nice table with a nice samovar.
And my mother used to bring the baked stuff and the milk.
And the milk was baked in the oven, so it had like a very thick top on it.
And I was always the first to see, because I never left my mother.
I ran always-- whatever she would do in the kitchen, I would be with her.
And when she used to wake up in the early mornings to bake for the market, for the restaurant, I used to go with her.
And she used to put on the table, and I saw and how the way she did it and what she did.
I never left her.
Only Polish.
And some Russian, and of course Yiddish was my, my mother's tongue.
It was affected because the Jews began-- they heard stories what went on in other places, and they became very frightened.
Our life at that time was not yet affected, because a very short time, it took on a few weeks, and the Russians occupied us.
But this is when our lives were affected.
Because the Russians considered us that we were bourgeois.
It means we were the more [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] richer people, in their eyes.
They took us out of that big house that we lived and put this in the back, where the two small homes were.
And our house became a big restaurant for the Russian people.
And we were very frightened, because my father didn't have any job.
My brothers didn't have also.
And although we had still food-- lots of food and grain and other things.
But I remember it was a very, very hard time.
And we thought that maybe it will, it will subside.
But the fright was never left us, because we were afraid [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that they will take us to Siberia.
But somehow, my brothers, my father, managed to bribe and so we stayed, and life went on.
I went to school, first grade.
And as the Russians were, they tried to-- everybody to ask to be a, to belong to their-- not to the army but to their organizations, like my sisters wanted to be pioneers or others.
But life wasn't dangerous.
We weren't-- we weren't afraid of our lives.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Only after they occupied us quite for a while-- I presume it was more than a year and a half or so-- that the war broke out.
The Germans attacked the Russians.
And at that time, really hell break-- broke out.
Everybody ran.
My brothers-- we had horses and buggies-- my brothers wanted very badly that we should run, too.
But my father thought that we suffered enough, and it wouldn't be prudent, it wouldn't be smart that we should also run with the Russians.
[SIGHS] The Germans came in.

[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And when everything really started, it took a very short while, maybe a month or so, that they tried to take most of the people from the vicinity and tried to organize a ghetto.
Not only the Germans, but the collaborators-- the people who were friends of all of us, supposedly-- became the police and the gendarme.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They did the ghetto.
They brought from everywhere, from all the farms that some Jews had farms, lived all around.
They brought them and the ghetto was organized in the place where our house stood.
So our houses were included in the ghetto.
They put barbed wire all around.
And this was already becoming-- this was already in the fall of I think of '42, no, maybe '41.
And-- yeah, it was in '41.
And it became-- it started to be cold.
They threw us in-- each family had only one room, we slept on top of each other.
It start being very cold.
We didn't have any wood or any fire to-- to warm ourselves.
And they didn't let the people bring many things when they collected them and just threw them into the ghetto.
Our whole family was together at the time.
But we took one of the biggers-- the bigger rooms.
We were all together.
At that time, my sister was married, had a boy, a baby.
And my other sister also got married, and she had a baby.
So we were-- our family became even larger.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And it, it was very, very cold.
And we were all very hungry.
As my mother used to have that big oven, they gave us some flour.
I mean, the Germans gave flour.
And my mother used to bake the bread for everybody.
She used to try to put in lots of water, so it would be a little bit more.
And then the bread was baked and she would-- not she, but it was a committee.
And they were dividing the bread to everybody, to every family.
But it started to be so dangerous.
Because first of all, they took the rabbis-- they took them to the synagogue-- and many older people.
They took them there, they cut off their beards.
They humiliated them.
They beat them so much that afterwards, when we knew that the people went there to see what went on, they couldn't be recognized.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This was one time.
This was the easy time.
Then they used to collect people who-- of, of intelligence, who knew something that it would be a threat, they thought they might be a threat.
They took them and they just killed them at random.
No, they took them out.
It was not far, it was like a little wooded area.
They killed them.
And then they start demanding all kinds of gold or belongings or thing.
And every time they had a quota that they had to deliver, and it-- if it wasn't delivered the quota they wanted, they took again the rabbis and the other religious people.
They took them to the synagogue, and they burned the synagogue and them.
And this was when the two synagogues was burned.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then they started to catch children.
They wanted only children.
My mother used to hide me under piles of things and-- because I was the smallest at that time.
But they still managed and they weed out many.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This-- to be in the ghetto was very, very hard.
The winter were fierce.
The hunger, it was impossible.
The things that we had was finished in a very short time, and they-- we didn't get any rations.
Only they gave us the flour that my mother used to bake.
And some people used to come out of the ghetto.
I mean, they would sneak out of the ghetto and go, and they would bring some kind of food, some kind of things.
And people used to get caught.
And of course, when you were caught, you were shot on the spot.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And this went on like that, until spring came.
And it--
Yes, they were taking the healthier people.
And they put them to dig for-- although it was winter.
But they put them to dig.
At that time, we didn't know why they were digging.
But this was quite a way from the city, and they dug big pits.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Some other people, younger people, women and other, work for the gendarmes.
They used to work in the kitchens, they used to work.
But at that time, I didn't.
And my-- I was with my mother.
But my sisters did work.
And also they worked, they had like seamstresses.
They were sewing, I don't know for whom, but they were working as seamstresses.
And cobblers were working, doing boots.
This, I understood, was done to send to the Germans.
They were provided with materials.
And they were doing them.
But that's all that I can really remember.
I-- mostly what I remember is the hunger, the cold, the misery, the-- the fright in everybody's eyes.
And many babies used to die, because the mothers didn't have any milk or any food.
And they just died, and so many, so many.
And they-- they were just buried, wherever we could.
Because we couldn't go to any cemetery.
They were just buried outside, like, like kittens, like-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] this what I can remember.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In June, they decided apparently that they would liquidate the ghetto.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Our house was-- we can look out from the windows, across was the, the police.
And we knew in the afternoon that commotion began, that many, many Germans came.
And also they brought from other places police and-- and so we know that something was going on.
But we didn't know exactly.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This night, my mother and I were in the window.
We didn't sleep.
And it was dawn.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We saw that they start coming out from that police building.
And they sort of divided like, in both sides.
So then we understood that they are going towards the ghetto from both sides.
And when they got closer, we let know the other people.
And the people start running towards the wooded area.
Some of the people cut, cut apart the wire.
And this is how we could start running.
My father begged us, all of us, that we should start running.
And we did.
He did not.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] He decided that he will stay.
[SOBS] And when the house was empty and the people were out, he put the house on fire.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I presume he perish within.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We start running.
It was summer.
It was warm.
Somehow, I was holding my sister's hand.
Her name is Celia.
We ran, we ran, we ran.
And of course, people were falling like flies.
So many people.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SIGHS] We ran.
I didn't see my mother or my sisters or anybody.
Somehow, I caught her hand, and I didn't let go.
And we ran.
We ran.
We came to a very thick, wooded area.
And we recognize it, because sometimes we would go with my parents, we would drive to visit certain Gentile people because of my father's involvement with buying up flax and other commodities.
When we stopped running, we were in a-- it was-- you could fall into it.
It was water, and you had to be frightened.
Because it was sink.
You would sink into it.
